title: RabbitMQ
purpose: Route messages through queues for reliable asynchronous workflows.
outcomes: Explain RabbitMQ concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important RabbitMQ features
Implement, test and debug practical RabbitMQ solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

stages.title: Fundamentals
stages.objective: Build an accurate mental model of RabbitMQ before relying on frameworks or memorized answers.

topics.title: Producer, exchange, queue and consumer
topics.definition: Producer, exchange, queue and consumer is a fundamentals part of RabbitMQ concerned with how engineers coordinate requests, business rules, data and integrations behind stable boundaries. It provides a vocabulary, operating model and constraints for making that part of a system deliberate rather than accidental.
topics.whyItMatters: Without understanding Producer, exchange, queue and consumer, a RabbitMQ solution may work on the happy path but become difficult to change, debug or operate. This topic makes ownership, behavior and failure conditions explicit.
whenToUse: Use Producer, exchange, queue and consumer when it directly supports a required RabbitMQ behavior
Use it after identifying the input, output, constraints and failure policy
Use it when the team can test and operate the resulting behavior
whenNotToUse: Do not add Producer, exchange, queue and consumer only because it is popular or appears in an architecture diagram
Avoid it when the added service or protocol creates more operational cost than the requirement justifies
Avoid abstraction before the repeated problem and ownership boundary are understood
internalWorking: Input enters the Producer, exchange, queue and consumer boundary and is validated against its contract
A request crosses routing, validation, domain logic, persistence and response boundaries
State changes should preserve a named invariant and produce an observable result
Failures propagate through a defined error, retry or recovery path
tradeoffs: Correctness and clarity versus implementation effort
Runtime performance versus memory or operational complexity
Flexibility versus a smaller, easier-to-test contract
commonMistakes: Memorizing the definition of Producer, exchange, queue and consumer without tracing an example
Ignoring invalid input, failure and cleanup behavior
Choosing an advanced implementation before measuring the simpler one

Define the contract, implement the happy path, reject invalid input and record enough context to diagnose failure.

topics.title: Prefetch and fairness
topics.definition: A promise represents the eventual completion or failure of asynchronous work. async/await provides sequential syntax while preserving promise-based execution.
topics.whyItMatters: Network and timer operations finish later; promises provide one composable value for success, failure and chaining.
whenToUse: Coordinate API requests and other non-blocking operations
Run independent operations concurrently with an explicit aggregate
Propagate cancellation and timeout signals
whenNotToUse: Awaiting independent operations one by one
Creating a promise around an already promise-based API
Ignoring rejection or component cleanup
internalWorking: Calling an async function immediately returns a promise
Await suspends that function, not the JavaScript thread
Promise reactions enter the microtask queue
The event loop runs microtasks after the current stack before the next task
tradeoffs: Readable sequential syntax versus hidden concurrency if awaits are misplaced
Promise.all is fast-fail and concurrent but needs a partial-failure policy
Cancellation is cooperative rather than automatic
commonMistakes: Forgetting to return or await a promise
Using array forEach with async callbacks
Updating UI from a stale request
topics.practicalExample: Load profile and roadmap concurrently with Promise.all, pass one AbortSignal to both, and show a recoverable error if either required request fails.

topics.title: Prefetch and fairness: applied patterns
topics.definition: A promise represents the eventual completion or failure of asynchronous work. async/await provides sequential syntax while preserving promise-based execution.
topics.whyItMatters: Network and timer operations finish later; promises provide one composable value for success, failure and chaining.
whenToUse: Coordinate API requests and other non-blocking operations
Run independent operations concurrently with an explicit aggregate
Propagate cancellation and timeout signals
whenNotToUse: Awaiting independent operations one by one
Creating a promise around an already promise-based API
Ignoring rejection or component cleanup
internalWorking: Calling an async function immediately returns a promise
Await suspends that function, not the JavaScript thread
Promise reactions enter the microtask queue
The event loop runs microtasks after the current stack before the next task
tradeoffs: Readable sequential syntax versus hidden concurrency if awaits are misplaced
Promise.all is fast-fail and concurrent but needs a partial-failure policy
Cancellation is cooperative rather than automatic
commonMistakes: Forgetting to return or await a promise
Using array forEach with async callbacks
Updating UI from a stale request
topics.practicalExample: Load profile and roadmap concurrently with Promise.all, pass one AbortSignal to both, and show a recoverable error if either required request fails.
